Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

- A CALIBRATED SIMULATION FOR OFFLINE TRAIN - ING OF REINFORCEMENT LEARNING AGENTS TO OP TIMIZE ENERGY AND EMISSION IN OFFICE BUILDINGS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Modern commercial Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) systems form a complex and interconnected thermodynamic system with the building and outside weather conditions, and current setpoint control policies are not fully optimized for minimizing energy use and carbon emission. Given a suitable training environment, a Reinforcement Learning (RL) model is able to improve upon these policies, but training such a model, especially in a way that scales to thousands of buildings, presents many practical challenges. To address these challenges, we propose a novel simulation based approach, where a customized simulator is used to train the agent for each building. Our simulator is lightweight and calibrated with recorded data from the building to achieve sufficient fidelity. On a two-story, 68,000 square foot building, with 127 devices, we were able to calibrate our simulator to have just over half a degree of drift from the real world over a 6 hour period. We train an RL agent on this simulator and demonstrate that our agent is able to learn an improved policy. This approach is an important step toward having a real-world Reinforcement Learning control system that can be scaled to many buildings, allowing for greater efficiency and resulting in reduced energy consumption and carbon emissions.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Energy optimization and management in commercial buildings is a very important problem, whose importance is only growing with time. Buildings account for 39% of all US carbon emissions (Lu & Lai, 2020). Reducing those emissions by even a small percentage can have a significant effect. In climates that are either very hot or very cold, energy consumption is much higher, and there is even more room to have a major impact.

Most office buildings are equipped with advanced HVAC devices, like Variable Air Volume (VAV) devices, Hot Water Systems, Air Conditioner and Air Handlers that are configured and tuned by the engineers, manufacturers, installers, and operators to run efficiently with the device’s local control loops McQuiston et al. (2023). However, integrating multiple HVAC devices from diverse vendors into a building “system” requires technicians to program fixed operating conditions for these units, which may not be optimal for every building and every potential weather condition. Existing setpoint control policies are not optimal under all conditions, and the possibility exists that a machine learning model may be trained to continuously tune a small number of setpoints to achieve greater energy efficiency and reduced carbon emission.

Our contributions include a highly customizable and scalable HVAC and building simulator, a rapid configuration method to customize the simulator to a particular building, a calibration method to improve this fidelity using real world data, and an evaluation method to measure the simulator fidelity. We also tran an RL agent on the simulator using Soft Actor Critic (Haarnoja et al., 2018), and demonstrate that we can learn an improved policy. This system enables offline learning, where the agent can train in an efficient sandbox environment that adequately emulates the dynamics of the building before being deployed on the real building, an important step towards a real world RL HVAC control system. We first present the simulator, discuss our configuration and calibration tech-

## 2 OPTIMIZING ENERGY AND EMISSION IN OFFICE BUILDINGS WITH REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

In this section we frame this energy optimization problem in office buildings as a Reinforcement Learning problem (RL). We define the state of the office building St at time t as a fixed length vector of measurements from sensors on the building’s devices, such as a specific VAV’s zone air temperature, gas meter’s flow rate, etc. The action on the building At is a fixed-length vector of device setpoints selected by agent at time t, such as the boiler supply water temperature setpoint, etc. We also define a custom feedback signal, or reward, _Rt_ ( _St, At, v_ ) that indicates the quality of taking action _At_ in state _St_ , as a weighted sum of negative cost functions for carbon emission, energy consumption, and zone-level setpoint deviation.

More generally, Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a branch of machine learning that attempts to train an agent to choose the best actions to maximize some long-term, cumulative reward (Sutton & Barto, 2018). As depicted below, the agent observes the state _St_ from the environment at time _t_ , then chooses action _At_ . The environment responds by transitioning to the next state _St_ + 1 and returns a reward (or penalty) after the action, _Rt_ + 1. Over time, the agent will explore the action space and learn to maximize the reward over the long term for each given state. A discount factor 1 reduces the value of future rewards amplifying the value of the near-term reward. When this cycle is repeated over multiple episodes, the agent converges on a state-action policy that maximizes the long-term reward.

This sequence is often formalized as the Markov Decision Process (MDP), described by the tuple ( _S, A, p, R_ ) where the state space is continuous (e.g., temperatures, flow rates, etc.) and the action space is continuous (e.g., setpoint temperatures) and the transition probability _p_ : _S ×S ×A →_ [0 _,_ 1] represents the probability density of the next state _St_ + 1 from taking action _At_ on the current state _St_ . The reward function _R_ : _S × A →_ [ _Rmin, Rmax_ ] emits a single scalar value at each time _t_ . The agent is acting under a policy ( _At|St_ ) which represents the policy of taking action _At_ from state _St_ . The goal of reinforcement learning is to find the policy that maximizes the expected long-term cumulative reward. The set of parameters _**θ**[∗]_ of the optimal policy can be expressed as:

_**θ**[∗]_ = arg max _θ_ E _τ ∼πθ_ ( _τ_ ) [[�] _t[γ][t][R]_[(] _[S][t][, A][t]_[)]]

where is the current policy parameter, and is a trajectory of states, actions, and rewards over multiple time steps _t_ . In order to converge to the optimal policy, the agent requires many training iterations, making training directly on the building from scratch inefficient, impracticable, if not impossible. Therefore, it is necessary to enable offline learning, where the agent can train in an efficient sandbox environment that adequately emulates the dynamics of the building before being deployed on the actual building.

## 3 RELATED WORKS

Considerable attention has been paid to HVAC control (Fong et al., 2006) in recent years (Kim et al., 2022), and a growing portion of that has considered how Reinforcement Learning and its various associated algorithms can be leveraged (Yu et al., 2021; Mason & Grijalva, 2019; Yu et al., 2020; Gao & Wang, 2023; Wang et al., 2023; V´azquez-Canteli & Nagy, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b; Fang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019b). As mentioned above, a central requirement in RL is the offline environment that trains the RL agent. Several methods have been proposed, largely falling under three broad categories.

**Data-driven Emulators** : Some works attempt to learn a dynamics as a multivariate regression model from real world data (Zou et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019a), often using recurrent neural network architecture, such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) (Velswamy et al., 2017; SendraArranz & Guti´errez, 2020; Zhuang et al., 2023). The difficulty here is that data-driven models often do not generalize well to circumstances outside the training distribution, especially since they are not physics based.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

**Offline RL** : The second approach is to train the agent directly from the historical real world data, without ever producing an interactive environment (Chen et al., 2020; 2023; Blad et al., 2022). While the real world data is obviously of high accuracy and quality, this presents a major challenge, since the agent cannot take actions in the real world and interact with any form of an environment, severely limiting its ability to improve over the baseline policy producing the real world data (Levine et al., 2020).

**Physics-based Simulation** : HVAC system simulation has long been studied (Trˇcka & Hensen, 2010; Riederer, 2005; Park et al., 1985; Trˇcka et al., 2009; Husaunndee et al., 1997; Trcka et al., 2007). EnergyPlus (Crawley et al., 2001), a high-fidelity simulator developed by the Department of Energy, is commonly used (Wei et al., 2017; Azuatalam et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2015; Wani et al., 2019; Basarkar, 2011), but suffers from the scalability issues outlined above.

To overcome the limitations of each of the above three methods, some work has proposed a hybrid approach (Zhao et al., 2021; Balali et al., 2023), and indeed this is the category our work falls under. What is unique about our approach is the use of a physics based simulator that achieves an ideal balance between speed and fidelity, which is sufficient to train an effective control agent off-line.

Various works have also discussed how exactly to apply RL to an HVAC environment, such as what sort of agent to train. Inspired by prior effective use of Soft Actor Critic on related problems (Kathirgamanathan et al., 2021; Coraci et al., 2021; Campos et al., 2022; Biemann et al., 2021), we chose to use a SAC agent.

## 4 SIMULATOR DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS

A fundamental tradeoff when designing a simulator is speed versus fidelity. Fidelity is the simulator’s ability to reproduce the building’s true dynamics that affect the optimization process. Speed minimizes both simulator configuration time, i.e., the time required to configure a simulator for a target building, and the agent training time, i.e., the time necessary for the agent to optimize its policy using the simulator.

Every building is unique, due to its physical layout, equipment, and location. Fully customizing a high fidelity simulation to a specific target building requires nearly exhaustive knowledge of the building structure, materials, location, etc., some of which are unknowable, especially for legacy office buildings. This requires manual “guestimation”, which can erode the accuracy promised by high-fidelity simulation. In general, the configuration time required for high-fidelity simulations limits their utility for deploying RL-based optimization to many buildings. High-fidelity simulations also are affected by computational demand and long execution times.

Alternatively, we developed a fast, low-to-medium-fidelity simulation model that was useful in addressing various design decisions, such as the reward function, and the modeling of different algorithms and for end-to-end testing. The simulation is built on a 2D finite-difference (FD) grid that models thermal diffusion, and a simplified HVAC model that generates or removes heat on special “diffuser” control volumes in the FD grid. For more details on design considerations, see Appendix C.

While the uncalibrated simulator is of low-to-medium fidelity, the key additional factor is data. We collect real world observations from the building we are attempting to simulate, and use that data to finetune the simulator. We believe this approach hits the sweet spot in this tradeoff, allowing us for scalability, while maintaining a high enough level of fidelity to be useful.

## 5 A LIGHTWEIGHT, CALIBRATED SIMULATION

Our goal is to develop a method for applying Reinforcement Learning at scale to commercial buildings. To this end, we put forth the following requirements for this to be feasible: We must have an easily customizable simulated environment to train the agent, with high enough fidelity to be useful. To meet this requirement, we designed a light weight simulator based on finite differences approximation of heat exchange. We proposed a simple automated procedure to go from building floor plans to a custom simulator in a short time, and we designed a fine tuning and evaluation pipeline,

In this thermal cycle, we highlight significant energy consumers as follows. The boiler burns natural gas to heat the water, _Q_[˙] _b_ . Water pumps consume electricity _W_[˙] _b,p_ to circulate heating water through the VAVs. The air handler fans consume electricity _W_[˙] _b,in_ , _W_[˙] _b,out_ to circulate the air through the VAVs. A motor drives the chiller’s compressor to operate a refrigeration cycle, consuming electricity _W_[˙] _c_ . We selected **water supply temperature** _T_[ˆ] _b_ and the **air handler supply temperature** _T_[ˆ] _s_ as agent actions because they affect the balance of electricity and natural gas consumption, they affect multiple device interactions, and they affect occupant comfort.

**Finite Differences Approximation** The diffusion of thermal energy in time and space of the building can be approximated using the method of Finite Differences (FD)Sparrow (1993); Lomax et al. (2002), and applying an energy balance. This method divides each floor of the building into a grid of three-dimensional control volumes and applies thermal diffusion equations to estimate the temperature of each control volume. By assuming each floor is adiabatically isolated, (i.e., no heat is transferred between floors), we can simplify the three-spatial dimensions into a spatial two-dimensional heat transfer problem. Each control volume is a narrow volume bounded horizontally, parameterized by ∆ _x_[2] , and vertically by the height of the floor. We note that heat transfer using air circulation is effectively the exchange of air mass between control volumes, which we approximate by a randomized shuffling of air within thermal zones, parameterized by a shuffle probability.

**Simulator Configuration** For RL to scale to many buildings, it is critical to be able to easily and rapidly configure the simulator to any arbitrary building. We designed a procedure that enabled a single technician, given floorplans and HVAC layout information, to generate a fully specified simulation in under three hours. In the interest of space, the details are provided in Appendix B.

**Simulator Calibration and Evaluation** In order to calibrate the simulator to the real world using data, we must have a metric with which to evaluate our simulator, and an optimization method to improve our simulator on this metric.

**N-Step Evaluation** We proposed a novel evaluation procedure, based on N-step prediction. Each iteration of our simulator was designed to represent a 5 minute interval, and our real world data is also obtained in five-minute intervals. To evaluate the simulator, we take a chunk of real data of _N_ observations. We then initialize the simulator so that its initial state matches that of the starting observation, and run the simulator for _N_ steps, replaying the same HVAC policy as was used in the real world observations. At this point, we calculate our simulation fidelity metric, which is the mean absolute error of the temperatures in each temperature sensor, at the final timestep. ## **Hyperparameter Calibration**

Once we defined our simulation fidelity metric, the spatial mean absolute temperature error, we can attempt to minimize this error, thus improving fidelity, by hyperparameter tuning several physical constants and other variables.

The parameters varied during the experiment included:

- Forced convection coefficient quantifying outside wind and air currents against the building exterior surfaces.

- Thermal conductivity, heat capacity, and density for exterior and interior walls.

- Shuffle Probability that approximates internal air circulation and interior forced convection.

## 6 EXPERIMENT RESULTS

We now demonstrate the results of how our simulator, when tuned and calibrated, is able to make useful real world predictions.

**Experiment Setup** To test out our simulator, we obtained data on our pilot building, a commercial office building located in northern California. The building has two stories with a combined surface area of 68,000 square feet, and has 127 HVAC devices. We obtained floor plan blueprints and used them to configure a customized simulator for the building, a process that took a single human less than three hours to complete.

**Calibration Data** To calibrate our simulator, we took nine chunks of observations. Four were used to tune the simulator, and the remaining five as validation of the tuned performance on unseen data. All times are given in US Pacific, the local time of the real building. All chunks of time were for 6 hours ( _N_ -step prediction, where _N_ = 72), with the start time listed in table 1.

**Calibration Procedure** We ran hyperparameter tuning for 100 iterations on our simulator. Below are the parameters varied, the ranges given, and the values found that best minimized the calibration metric. The metric was calculated by obtaining the Mean Absolute Spatial Error of temperatures on each of the four train scenarios, and then averaging them.
By including multiple scenarios in our tuning process, we ensured that our hyperparamaters did not overfit to any specific scenario.

We reviewed the physical constants that yielded the lowest simulation error from calibration. Densities, heat capacities, and conductivities plausibly matched common interior and exterior building materials. However, the external convection coefficient was higher than under the weather conditions, and likely is compensating for the radiative losses and gains, which were not directly simulated.

## **Calibration Results**

We present the predictive results of our calibrated simulator, on _N_ -step prediction, where _N_ = 72, representing a 6 hour predictive window. We calculated the spatial mean absolute temperature error, as defined above. We also present a second metric, the median spatial temperature error. This was not used in the tuning process, but gives us some insight into how well the calibration process is performing.

As indicated in Table 3, our tuning procedure drifts only 0 _._ 58 degrees on average over a six hour period on the tuning set, and we get good generalization, average test error is only slightly larger at 0 _._ 83. One interesting point is that the performance on scenario _S_ 6 and _S_ 9 were so different, despite being the same time of day. We believe this is due to the differing weather conditions on those two days affecting our model differently. It should be noted that an uncalibrated model (ie our baseline) had a much larger mean error of 1 _._ 97 degrees.

**Visualizing Temperature Drift Over Time** Figure 3 illustrates temperature drift over time for scenario _S_ 6. At each time step, we calculate the spatial temperature error for all sensors, and present the errors as a boxplot distribution.

In this case, this figure is very useful in helping us understand why the error in scenario _S_ 6 was larger than the others. The scenario began at 4:40 AM, and at 7:AM, two hours and twenty minutes in, the building shifted from night mode to day mode. As can be observed in the figure, at the two hour twenty minute mark, the simulator began drifting from the real world, becoming a biased estimator.

The larger error here was caused by our simulator being unable to correctly predict the transition from night mode to day mode. For similar visuals on the other scenarios, see appendix A.
**Visualizing Spatial Errors** Figure 4 illustrates the results of this predictive process over a six hour period, on scenario _S_ 4.

Figure 4.a displays a heatmap of the spatial temperature difference throughout the building, between the real world and simulator, after three hours. Red indicates that in that location, the simulator was warmer than the real world, and blue indicates colder. White means that the simulator and real world were the same temperature in that location. Figure 4.b illustrates the same after six hours. There are several key takeaways from this illustration. The ring of blue around the building indicates that our simulator is too cold on the perimeter, which implies that the heat exchange with the outside is happening more rapidly than it would in the real world. The inside of the building remains red, which means that despite the simulator perimeter being cooler than the real world, the inside is warmer. This implies that our thermal exchange within the building is not as rapid as that of the real world. We suspect that this may be because our simulator does not have a radiative heat transfer model. Lastly, there is a band of white around the perimeter of the building, inside the band of blue and surrounding the red areas. These are the locations where the temperature reached the correct equilibrium, despite too much exterior exchange, and not enough air moving around inside. These locations were the right distance within the building to be the exact same as in the real world data.

As mentioned above, we suspect the culprit is a missing radiative heat model that drives the interior exchange to be too low. To cope with this, the hyper parameter tuning process chooses a too high exterior exchange. These insights can help improve the fidelity of the simulator in the future. For similar visuals of the test scenarios, see appendix A. For a more elaborate version of this visual also including the differences after three hours for comparison, see appendix E.

## 7 DISCUSSION AND FUTURE WORK

While developing a realistic simulator and training an agent is an important step, our long term goal is to successfully deploy an agent to the real world. Thus, our aim for the future is real world transfer, demonstrating that an agent trained using our simulation procedure can indeed transfer to a real building. We already have a real building that we have the ability to control, and using our pipeline produced a simulator that perfectly matches all of its devices, setpoints and sensors. We also have a method of tuning this simulator, based on real data, to make higher accuracy predictions. Our future goal, showing how an agent, trained on this simulator, is able to produce a useful policy on the real building, will require a few more components. In future live trials on the real building, we will train an RL agent on our simulator, and carefully measure its performance in the real world. We also have outlined several approaches that we intend to use to further improve our simulator and fine tuning process
1. Tuning on data from multiple seasons, various times of day, and longer time windows, to improve generalizability and reduce overfitting.

2. Updating our simulation by adding in a radiative heat transfer model to help with interior heat exchange, and by increasing the action space to include air handler and water system device on/off commands and air handler static pressure and hot water system differential pressure.
3. Adding a Neural Network based predictor corrector to the tuning process to further improve fidelity.
In addition, in the future we hope to incorporate an occupancy model (Peng et al., 2018) to give the agent specific knowledge of where the building occupants are located.
We are optimistic that our novel simulation tuning process will allow us to develop a useful, and most importantly, transferable, HVAC control solution, and we hope our work will inspire further

## B SIMULATOR CONFIGURATION PROCEDURE DETAILS

To configure the simulator, we require two type of information on the building:

1. Floorplan blueprints. This includes the size and shapes of rooms and walls for each floor.

2. HVAC metadata. This includes each device, its name, location, setpoints, fixed parameters and purpose.

We preprocess the detailed floorplan blueprints of the building, and extract a grid that gives us an approximate placement of walls and how rooms are divided. This is done via the following procedure:

1. Using threshold _t_ , binarize the floorplan image into a grid of 0s and 1s.

2. Find and replace any large features that need to be removed (such as doors, a compass, etc)
3. Iteratively apply standard binary morphology operations (erosion and dilation) to the image to remove noise from background, while preserving the walls.

4. Resize the image, such that each pixel represents exactly one control volume

5. Run a connected components search to determine which control volumes are exterior to the building, and mark them accordingly
6. Run a DFS over the grid, and reduce every wall we encounter to be only a single control volume thick in the case of interior wall, and double for exterior wall

Figure 16: Before and after images of the floorplan preprocessing algorithm

We also employ a simple user interface to label the location of each HVAC device on the floorplan grid. This information is passed into our simulator, and a custom simulator for the new building, with roughly accurate HVAC and floor layout information, is created. This allows us to then calibrate this simulator using the real world data, which will now match the simulator in terms of device names and locations.

We tested this pipeline on our pilot building, which consisted of two floors with combined surface area of 68,000 square feet, and has 127 HVAC devices. Given floorplans and HVAC layout information, a single technician was able to generate a fully specified simulation in under three hours. This customized simulator matched the real building in every device, room, and structure.

## C SIMULATOR DESIGN CONSIDERATION DETAILS

A simulator models the physical system dynamics of the building, devices, and external weather conditions, and can train the control agent interactively, if the following desiderata are achieved:

1. The simulation must produce the same observation dimensionality as the actual real building. In other words, each device-measurement present in the real building must also be present in the simulation.

2. The simulation must accept the same actions (device-setpoints) as the real building.

3. The simulation must return the reward input data described above (zone air temperatures, energy use, and carbon emission).
4. The simulation must propagate, estimate, and compute the thermal dynamics of the actual real building and generate a state update at each timestep.

5. The simulation must model the dynamics of the HVAC system in the building, including thermostat response, setpoints, boiler, air conditioning, water circulation, and air circulation. This includes altering the HVAC model in response to a setpoint change in an action request.

6. The time required to recalculate a timestep must be short enough to train a viable agent in a reasonable amount of time. For example, if a new agent should be trained in under three days (259,200 seconds), requiring 500,000 steps, the average time required to update the building should be 0.5 seconds or less.
7. The simulator must be configurable to a target building with minimal manual effort. We believe our simulation system meets all of these listed requirements.

## D SIMULATION IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

## D.1 THERMAL MODEL FOR THE SIMULATION

As a template for a developing a large number of simulators, we propose a general-purpose highlevel thermal model for simulating office buildings, illustrated in Figure 2. The primary objective of the climate control system is to maintain the room temperature _Tz_ of each conditioned zone with in its heating and cooling setpoints[2] , _T_[ˆ] _z,min_ and _T_[ˆ] _z,max_ . The Variable Air Volume (VAV) devices feed the zones with continuous thermal energy[3] , _Q_[˙] _s_ and offset thermal losses or gains from windows, floors, people, computers and other heat-generating devices, etc. _Q_[˙] _z_ and the rate of thermal energy drawn out of the zone by recirculation _Q_[˙] _r_ . The VAVs regulate the supply airflow, _m_ ˙ _s_ , and the heating water flow, _m_ ˙ _b_ that heats the supply air to a desired temperature _Ts,out_ . Boilers supply VAVs with heating water, circulated by electrical water pumps. The air handlers supply multiple VAVs/zones with fresh and potentially cooled air, and regulate the proportion of recirculated and fresh air by setting dampers and running supply and exhaust electrical fans. When cooling is necessary, the air handler cools the supply air with electrically driven chillers and possible coolant pumps. In this thermal cycle, the following significant energy consumers are highlighted:

- The boiler burns natural gas to heat the water, _Q_[˙] _b_ .

- Water pumps consume electricity _W_[˙] _b,p_ to circulate heating water through the VAVs.
- The air handler fans consume electricity _W_[˙] _b,in_ , _W_[˙] _b,out_ to circulate the air through the VAVs.
- A motor drives the chiller’s compressor to operate a refrigeration cycle, consuming electricity _W_[˙] _c_ .
- In some buildings coolant is circulated through the air handlers with pumps that consume electricity, _W_[˙] _c,p_ .

All electrical consumers may be supplied by electricity obtained from renewable sources. However, the boiler consumes natural gas and contributes to greenhouse gas emissions. The cost of electricity varies by time and current load on the grid; prices are highest during peak demand periods.

## D.2 OPTIMIZATION TRADEOFFS

In a **heating condition** ( _Tamb < Tz,min_ ), heated air must be fed to the zones to compensate for thermal losses, such that _Ts,out > Tz_ , and the amount of heat applied is proportional to the mass ˙ flow rate times the difference in temperature, _Q_[˙] _s ∝ ms ×_ ( _Ts,out − Tz_ ). The agent can trade off air flow for temperature to get the desired heating. The air flow is governed by the fan use and damper configuration, and the supply air temperature is governed by the heating water temperature and the amount of heat applied to the boiler. In a cooling condition ( _Tamb > Tz,min_ ), heat must be removed from the zone by replacing higher temperature air with lower temperature air, _Ts,out < Tz_ . In a single-zone configuration, the problem is fairly simple: disable the boiler and engage the chiller until the zone temperature is within the setpoint. However, an air handler must service multiple zones simultaneously with different thermal gains. Some zones that have lower thermal gains, i.e. are colder and require less cooling, may require supplemental heating to ensure that all zones are within the setpoint ranges. Here, the tradeoff is how much cold air needs to be circulated to ensure all zones are within set point while minimizing the amount of heating.

## D.3 SETPOINTS AND ACTIONS

On its initialization, the environment queries the building for device setpoints. Every continuousvalued device setpoint is eligible to be acted upon by an agent action. However, it may not be desirable for the agent to control every possible setpoint reported by the building. Larger action spaces generally are more difficult to optimize than smaller action spaces, and because exploration of some setpoints may jeopardize occupancy comfort (e.g., zone air temperature setpoints), not all setpoints should be controlled by the agent (at least for the initial versions). Furthermore, some device setpoints are controlled by tuned equipment-level controls, and attempting to control these setpoints may affect the device’s ability to function properly.

We selected setpoints for actions based on the following criteria:

1. The setpoints should affect the balance of electricity and natural gas consumption.
2. The setpoints should affect device interactions (i.e., changing the setpoint on a device will force a change on another device).

3. Changes to the setpoint should have an indirect effect on occupant comfort.

Based on this criteria, we selected the **water supply temperature** and the **air handler supply temperature** as initial setpoints for agent actions. Since the target building has two primary air handler units and one hot water system, the action space dimensionality is 3.

## D.4 FINITE DIFFERENCES APPROXIMATION

The diffusion of thermal energy in time and space of the building can be approximated using the method of Finite Differences (FD)(Sparrow, 1993; Lomax et al., 2002), and applying an energy balance. This method divides each floor of the building into a grid of three-dimensional control volumes and applies thermal diffusion equations to estimate the temperature of each control volume. By assuming that each floor is adiabatically isolated, (i.e., no heat is transferred down or up between floors), we can simplify the three-spatial dimensions into a spatial two-dimensional heat transfer problem. Each control volume is a narrow volume bounded horizontally, parameterized by ∆ _x_[2] , and vertically by the height of the floor.

The energy balance, shown in Equation 1, is applied to each discrete control volume in the FD grid consists of the following components: (a) thermal exchange across each face of the four participating faces control volume via conduction or convection _Q_ 1, _Q_ 2, _Q_ 3, _Q_ 4, (b) the change in internal energy over time in the control volume _Mc_[∆] _[T]_[and][(c)][an][external][energy][source][that][enables][applying] ∆ _t_[,] local thermal energy from the HVAC model only for those control volumes that include an airflow diffuser, _Qext_ .
Where _M_ is the mass and _c_ is the heat capacity of the control volume, ∆ _T_ is the change in temperature from the previous time step and ∆ _t_ is the timestep interval.

The thermal exchange in (a) is calculated using Fourier’s law of steady conduction in the interior control volumes (walls and interior air), parameterized by the conductivity of the volume, and the exchange across the exterior faces of control volumes are calculated using the forced convection equation, parameterized by the convection coefficient, which approximates winds and currents sur-

rounding the building. The change in internal energy (b) is parameterized by the density, and heat capacity of the control volume. Finally, the thermal energy associated with the VAV (c) is equally distributed to all associated control volumes that have a diffuser.

Thermal diffusion within the building is mainly accomplished via forced or natural convection currents, which can be notoriously difficult to estimate accurately. We note that heat transfer using air circulation is effectively the exchange of air mass between control volumes, which we approximate by a randomized shuffling of air control volumes within thermal zones, parameterized by a shuffle probability.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical bottleneck in building-scale Reinforcement Learning (RL): the trade-off between simulation fidelity and scalability. The authors propose a "lightweight" calibrated simulator as a middle ground between computationally heavy physics engines (like EnergyPlus) and purely data-driven models that lack physical consistency. The primary strength of the work lies in its practical focus on real-world deployment challenges, specifically the claim of achieving a HVAC-specific calibration with only 0.5°F of drift over six hours across 127 devices—a significant engineering feat if validated. However, the paper’s novelty is currently obscured by a lack of technical specificity in the provided text. The "novel simulation-based approach" and "rapid configuration method" are mentioned as primary contributions, yet the underlying mathematical or architectural differences from existing Gray-Box modeling or RC (Resistance-Capacitance) modeling are not immediately clear. To meet the high bar of ICLR, the authors must ensure the "calibration method" is not merely a standard parameter-fitting exercise but offers a methodological contribution that solves the generalizability problem inherent in building simulations. Furthermore, while the transition to RL is standard, the ultimate success of the paper will depend on whether the authors can demonstrate that an agent trained on this *specific* calibrated simulator outperforms agents trained on standard models when transferred back to the real-world building (Sim2Real gap).

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Error in Policy Reward Equation
comments.explanation: The objective function for the optimal policy parameters θ* is mathematically malformed. Both the text and the subsequent formalisms (Paragraph 9) fail to use standard summation notation or clear power-based discounting (γ^t), instead using 't' as a coefficient/index in a way that suggests a product. Additionally, the expectation is taken over π_θ(τ) while π_θ was defined as an action distribution π_θ(A_t|S_t), creating a derivation gap for a reader relying on these formalisms.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Reported Simulation Drift and Units
comments.quote: 0.58 ^◦K (Table 3) vs. "just over half a degree" (Abstract) vs "0.83" (Prose)
comments.explanation: There is a significant inconsistency regarding the reported error metrics. Table 3 lists the training MAE as 0.58 °K, while the abstract refers to a drift of '~0.5°F'. Because a 0.58 °K difference is approximately 1.04 °F, the prose significantly understates the error magnitude compared to the data. Furthermore, while the prose claims a test error of 0.83, the 'Median' row in Table 3 contains negative values (e.g., -0.29), which is logically impossible for an Absolute Error (MAE) metric, suggesting the table actually displays bias (Mean Error) without clarifying the label.

comments.title: Misidentification of the Discount Factor
comments.quote: A discount factor 1 reduces the value of future rewards amplifying the value of the near-term reward.
comments.explanation: The text states the discount factor is '1'. In Reinforcement Learning, a discount factor (γ) of 1 means that future rewards are not reduced at all (undiscounted MDP), which contradicts the second half of the sentence describing the reduction of future reward values. For the stated behavior to occur, the factor must be 0 ≤ γ < 1. This inconsistency misleads the reader regarding the convergence properties used in the trainer.

comments.title: Ambiguous Reward Function Parameter
comments.quote: we also define a custom feedback signal, or reward, _Rt_ ( _St, At, v_ ) that indicates the quality of taking action _At_ in state _St_ , as a weighted sum of negative cost functions
comments.explanation: The reward function includes a parameter 'v' which is never defined in the text, the subsequent MDP tuple (S, A, p, R), or the expected reward formula. Without clarification on whether 'v' is a learned vector, a static weight for multi-objective optimization (carbon vs. comfort), or an environmental variable, the methodology remains underspecified and cannot be replicated.

comments.title: Dimensional and Physical Inconsistency in Energy Balance
comments.quote: $Qext + Q 1 + Q 2 + Q 3 + Q 4 = Mc[∆] ∆ [T]
comments.explanation: The energy balance equation is physically inconsistent. The left side (Q_1...Q_4) represents power (Watts), while the right side (McΔT) represents a change in total internal energy (Joules). For dimensional consistency, the right side must be a rate of change (Mc ΔT/Δt). Additionally, the horizontal boundaries are parameterized as Δx^2, which refers to an area; conduction faces in a 2D finite difference grid should be parameterized by side length and floor height (Δx · H). These errors compromise the validity of the temperature transition dynamics.

comments.title: Omission of Training/Simulator Consistency
comments.quote: This agent was not trained on a simulator using the hyperparameters obtained in the tuning process... but instead was trained on our simulator with default hyperparameters.
comments.explanation: The paper’s core premise is that high-fidelity simulator calibration (Section 5) is necessary for effective offline RL. However, Footnote 1 admits the RL agent shown in the results was trained on an uncalibrated simulator with 'default' hyperparameters. Since the default model error is significantly higher than the calibrated one (1.97 vs 0.58), this creates a logical gap: the paper fails to prove that its calibration contribution actually benefits the RL performance, which is the stated goal.

comments.title: Inconsistent Spatial MAE and Sensor Definitions
comments.quote: MAE_t = 1/Z Σ |T_real - T_sim| ... where T_sim is the mean of all control volumes
comments.explanation: The authors define T_sim as the average of all control volumes in a zone. This creates a representation error: real-world sensors (T_real) are typically localized (e.g., wall-mounted), whereas a zone-wide average includes boundary layer temperatures near exterior walls and windows. The paper does not specify if T_sim accounts for sensor placement or is restricted to air nodes, which is critical for claiming a sub-degree calibration accuracy relative to physical sensors.

comments.title: Unspecified 'Shuffle Probability' and 'Swap Radius'
comments.quote: we approximate by a randomized shuffling of air within thermal zones, parameterized by a shuffle probability.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'shuffle probability' and 'swap radius' as key hyperparameters to simulate convection without a 3D CFD model. However, the implementation is underspecified. It is unclear if this involves stochastic temperature swaps between adjacent cells or a zone-wide mixing. Without defining these mechanisms or the units of 'swap radius' (cells vs. meters), the simulation logic is irreproducible.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Evaluation Strategy (N-step vs. Drift)
comments.quote: calculate our simulation fidelity metric... at the final timestep.
comments.explanation: The fidelity metric is calculated based on the MAE at the final timestep (t=72) of a 6-hour trajectory. Evaluating only the endpoint is insufficient for 'fidelity' in dynamical systems, as it fails to account for oscillations or significant deviations that might occur mid-trajectory. Standard practice requires cumulative MAE or RMSE over the entire sequence to ensure the dynamics are captured correctly throughout the state-space.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Error in Policy Reward Equation
comments.explanation: In the formal definition of the optimal policy parameters θ*, the term for the discounted return is mathematically garbled. Standard RL notation (referenced in Sutton & Barto 2018) defines the return as the sum over time steps t of γ^t * R_t. The text shows a character '' (likely an OCR error for the summation symbol ∑) followed by 't[γ][t]'. If this were interpreted as t multiplied by γ, it would deviate from standard MDP discounted reward formulations. Furthermore, the use of 't' as both a coefficient and an exponent, or as a subscript for γ, is non-standard. While I acknowledge the OCR warning, 't[γ][t]' is a structural notation error that fails to clearly represent the geometric discounting factor γ^t or the summation over t.

In Reinforcement Learning, a discount factor (γ) of 1 means that future rewards are not reduced at all (undiscounted MDP), which contradicts the second half of the sentence: 'reduces the value of future rewards'. For a discount factor to reduce the value of future rewards and amplify the importance of near-term ones, it must be the case that 0 ≤ γ < 1. Stating the factor is '1' while describing the behavior of a factor less than 1 is a logical inconsistency that could mislead a reader about the convergence properties of the trainer.

comments.title: Ambiguous Reward Function Parameter
comments.quote: we also define a custom feedback signal, or reward, _Rt_ ( _St, At, v_ ) that indicates the quality of taking action _At_ in state _St_ , as a weighted sum of negative cost functions
comments.explanation: The reward function is introduced with three parameters: S_t, A_t, and 'v'. While S_t and A_t are standard (state and action), 'v' is never defined in the text or the subsequent MDP tuple (S, A, p, R). In the context of building optimization, 'v' might represent a vector of weights for the multi-objective cost function (carbon, energy, comfort), but its exclusion from the formal MDP definition (S, A, p, R) and the subsequent expected reward formula makes the method underspecified. A reader cannot determine if 'v' is a learned parameter, a stochastic environmental variable, or a static weight vector.

comments.title: Dimensional Inconsistency in FD Discretization
comments.quote: Each control volume is a narrow volume bounded horizontally, parameterized by ∆ _x_[2] , and vertically by the height of the floor.
comments.explanation: The authors state they are simplifying a 3D heat transfer problem into a 2D spatial grid by assuming floors are adiabatically isolated. However, they parameterize the horizontal boundary as $\Delta x^2$. In a standard finite difference (FD) energy balance for a 2D grid, the horizontal dimensions are typically $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$. If the grid is uniform and square, the area of a top-down face would be $\Delta x^2$, but the 'horizontal boundary' (the faces through which conduction $Q_1$ through $Q_4$ occurs) should be the product of a side length and the floor height $H$ (i.e., $\Delta x \cdot H$). If $\Delta x^2$ is intended to represent the surface area of a control volume, it contradicts the physical description of conduction between adjacent cells in a 2D grid. This makes the subsequent energy balance equation difficult to verify for dimensional consistency.

comments.title: Missing Temperature Normalization in MAE Definition
comments.quote: $T_{sim,t,z} = C_1 z \sum_{Cc=1}^z [T][t,c]$
comments.explanation: The prose defines $T_{sim,t,z}$ as the 'mean temperature of all control volumes in zone $z$'. However, the formula provided (likely OCR-mangled but discernibly $C_1 z \sum T_{t,c}$) suggests a summation. For this to be a 'mean', the formula should be $1/N_z \sum T_{t,c}$ where $N_z$ is the count of control volumes in that zone. While I considered if $C_1 z$ was an OCR error for $1/|C_z|$, the current notation is ambiguous. Furthermore, if the zones have vastly different numbers of control volumes, an unnormalized sum would lead to a mathematically incorrect mean, causing the MAE calculation for the whole building to be weighted incorrectly toward larger zones.

comments.title: Ambiguous Energy Balance Equation
comments.quote: $Qext + Q 1 + Q 2 + Q 3 + Q 4 = Mc[∆] ∆ [T] t[, where] [ M]$ is the mass and $[ c]$ is the heat capacity
comments.explanation: The energy balance equation as written is $Q = Mc \Delta T$. In heat transfer physics, $Q$ (the heat flow rates $Q_1...Q_4$) represents power (Watts, or Joules/sec). The right side of the equation $Mc \Delta T$ represents a change in total internal energy (Joules). For the equation to be physically sound, the right side must be the rate of change of internal energy, typically $Mc \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$. While the text mentions $\Delta t$ is the timestep interval, the placement of $\Delta t$ in the OCR text ($Mc[\Delta] \dots [\text{and}] (c) \dots \Delta t$) is fragmented. If the agent is using this simulator to learn dynamics, a confusion between Energy and Power in the fundamental FD equation would lead to incorrect temperature transitions unless $\Delta t$ is exactly 1 unit of time, which contradicts the '5 minute interval' specified later.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Evaluation Strategy (N-step vs. Drift)
comments.quote: At this point, we calculate our simulation fidelity metric, which is the mean absolute error of the temperatures in each temperature sensor, at the final timestep.
comments.explanation: The authors define their fidelity metric based on the MAE at the *final* timestep ($t=N-1$) of a 6-hour (72-step) trajectory. Later, in the abstract and results, they claim a drift of ~0.5°F. However, evaluating only the final timestep of a long-run simulation can be misleading. A simulation could oscillate wildly or deviate significantly at $t=36$ but happen to cross the real-world value again at $t=72$. Standard practice for 'fidelity' in RL environments usually involves the cumulative MAE over the entire $N$ steps or the root mean square error (RMSE) across the trajectory to ensure the dynamics are captured throughout the state-space transition, not just the endpoint. Relying solely on the final step for calibration might lead the SAC agent to learn policies that exploit 'lucky' endpoint alignments that don't reflect true thermodynamic trends.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Reported Simulation Drift
comments.quote: our tuning procedure drifts only 0 . 58 degrees on average over a six hour period on the tuning set, and we get good generalization, average test error is only slightly larger at 0 . 83.
comments.explanation: There appears to be a numerical discrepancy between the text and the data presented in Table 3. The prose states the average test error is 0.83. However, looking at Table 3, the individual test scenarios are S5 (0.45), S6 (1.22), S7 (0.90), S8 (0.65), and S9 (0.94). The arithmetic mean of these five values is 0.832, which matches. However, the 'Test Mean' row in Table 3 actually lists the value '0.83' on the MAE line but then follows it with 'NA' and various offset values. Furthermore, the Abstract claims a drift of 'just over half a degree... over a 6 hour period,' which matches the training MAE (0.58) but understates the expected performance on unseen data (0.83). While the error is small, the inconsistent representation of 'Mean' vs 'Median' in Table 3 makes it difficult to verify the '0.83' figure as a robust metric of simulator fidelity.

comments.title: Ambiguous Units and Values in Table 3
comments.explanation: In Table 3, the row labeled 'Median' contains several negative values (e.g., -0.29, -0.49). Earlier in Equation 1, the paper defines the evaluation metric as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), which by definition cannot be negative. If the authors intended to show 'Median Error' (bias) rather than 'Median Absolute Error,' the label is misleading. Additionally, the text in the abstract and results uses Fahrenheit (°F) and Kelvin (°K) interchangeably or inconsistently. The paper summary mentions '0.5°F drift', but Table 3 uses '◦K'. While a 1:1 change in Kelvin is equal to a 1:1 change in Celsius, it is not equal to a change in Fahrenheit. This creates ambiguity regarding the actual physical precision of the simulator calibration.

comments.title: Omission of Training/Simulator Consistency
comments.quote: This agent was not trained on a simulator using the hyperparameters obtained in the tuning process, due to time constraints, but instead was trained on our simulator with default hyperparameters.
comments.explanation: The central claim of the paper is that a *calibrated* simulator is necessary and effective for offline RL training. However, the authors admit in Footnote 1 that the RL results presented in Figure 5 use the uncalibrated (default) simulator. This creates a logical gap in the proof-of-concept: the paper demonstrates that a simulator can be calibrated (Section 5) and that an RL agent can learn in a simulator (Section 6), but it fails to demonstrate that the *calibration* actually improves the RL agent's performance or that the agent learned in Section 6 would be viable in the real building. Given the statement that the uncalibrated model had a significantly higher error (1.97 vs 0.58), an agent trained on default parameters may overfit to the simulator's physics errors.

comments.title: Plausibility of Convection Coefficients
comments.quote: convection coeffcient (W/m2/K) ... best 255
comments.explanation: In Table 2, the 'best' hyperparameter for the exterior convection coefficient is listed as 255 W/m²/K. In standard heat transfer literature, forced convection coefficients for air typically range from 10 to 100 W/m²/K, even in high-wind conditions. A value of 255 is exceptionally high for atmospheric conditions. The authors acknowledge this in the text, suggesting it compensates for missing radiative models. While this is a known technique in 'grey-box' modeling, labeling it a 'physical constant' that 'plausibly matched' materials is a stretch. More importantly, using such an inflated value to compensate for radiation might lead to poor generalization across different seasons (e.g., high-sun vs. low-sun days), which might explain the high variance seen in scenarios S6 and S9.

comments.title: Inconsistent formulation of the RL optimization objective
comments.quote: ${\mathbf{\theta}}^{[*]} = \text{arg max}_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta(\tau)} [ [\dots] t[\gamma][t][R][(] [[S][t][, A][t]]_[)]]$
comments.explanation: The objective function for $\theta^*$ in the text contains significant notation errors that go beyond simple OCR misreads. While the summary correctly identifies the intent as $\mathbb{E}_{\tau \in \pi_\theta} [ \sum_t \gamma^t R(S_t, A_t) ]$, the text in Section 2 is garbled. Specifically, it uses 't' inside brackets in a way that suggests a product or index rather than a power ($\gamma^t$) or a summation index. Furthermore, the expectation is taken over $\pi_\theta(\tau)$, but $\pi_\theta$ is earlier defined as $\pi_\theta(A_t|S_t)$. While a trajectory distribution $p_\theta(\tau)$ is standard, the expression here is mathematically malformed (e.g., '$[[\dots] t[\gamma][t]$'). Even assuming 't' is the summation index, the lack of a summation symbol $\Sigma$ in the main text's equation makes the derivation of $\theta^*$ technically incomplete for a reader relying on the paper's formalisms.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Spatial MAE definition
comments.quote: $\text{MAE}_t = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{z=1}^Z |T_{real,t,z} - T_{sim,t,z}| \dots \text{where} \ T_{sim,t,z} = \frac{1}{C_z} \sum_{c=1}^{C_z} T_{t,c}$
comments.explanation: The authors define the simulation temperature for a zone ($T_{sim,t,z}$) as the mean of all control volumes $C_z$ in that zone. However, the Finite Difference model described in Section 5 ('Finite Differences Approximation') treats the floor as a 2D grid where only specific control volumes contain an 'airflow diffuser'. In real buildings, temperature sensors (which provide $T_{real,t,z}$) are usually wall-mounted or located in specific return air paths, not averaged across every cubic inch of the zone's volume (including air near windows/walls). If $T_{sim,t,z}$ is a simple average of all CVs, it likely includes boundary layer temperatures near exterior walls which would bias the error metric compared to a thermostat reading. The text doesn't specify if the average is restricted to 'air nodes' or if it accounts for sensor placement, which is a key requirement for the 'calibration' claim of 0.5°F accuracy.

comments.title: Inconsistent units for calibration results
comments.quote: 0.58 $^\circ K$ (Table 3) vs. "just over half a degree of drift" (Abstract)
comments.explanation: The Abstract and Key Claims section state the drift is "just over half a degree" or "~0.5$^{\circ}$F". In Table 3, the Training Data MAE is listed as 0.58 $^{\circ}K$. A difference of 0.5 degrees Fahrenheit is approximately 0.28 Kelvin. If the error is 0.58$^{\circ}K$, that is actually ~1.04$^{\circ}F$. While the numerical performance is still impressive, there is a literal inconsistency between the Kelvin-based metrics in the tables and the Fahrenheit-based claims in the prose. This is potentially misleading regarding the precision of the calibration.

comments.title: Unspecified 'Shuffle Probability' mechanism
comments.quote: we approximate by a randomized shuffling of air within thermal zones, parameterized by a shuffle probability.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'shuffle probability' as a critical hyperparameter to solve the difficulty of estimating forced/natural convection. However, the mechanism is underspecified. It is unclear if this involves swapping the temperature values of two random cells within a zone at each timestep, or a stochastic mixing of all cells. Since this parameter is used to compensate for the lack of a proper 3D CFD model, the lack of detail on how the 'swap radius' (mentioned in Table 2) and 'shuffle probability' interact makes the simulation logic irreproducible. For example, does a 'swap radius' of 50 mean 50 grid cells, or 50 meters? Without this, the 'rapid configuration' claim is hard to verify.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-structured and timely contribution to the application of reinforcement learning (RL) in real-world energy optimization problems, specifically focusing on HVAC control in commercial buildings. The motivation is strong, grounded in the significant carbon footprint of buildings and the limitations of current heuristic control policies. The authors propose a practical, scalable framework for offline RL training via a lightweight, calibrated building simulator—an important step toward deployable, data-efficient RL systems in safety- and cost-sensitive environments. The overall clarity is high, with a logical flow from problem formulation to methodology and preliminary results. Technical concepts are introduced with appropriate formalism, particularly in framing the control task as a Markov Decision Process and defining reward components relevant to energy, emissions, and comfort. However, the most significant issue lies in the incomplete development and justification of the calibration method: while the claim of "just over half a degree of drift over 6 hours" suggests high fidelity, the paper does not yet clarify how this calibration is achieved, what data it relies on, or how generalizable the approach is across buildings with heterogeneous systems or varying data quality. Additionally, the related work section cuts off mid-sentence, indicating an oversight in submission preparation. The absence of ablation studies or comparison with existing simulation approaches (e.g., EnergyPlus, data-driven emulators) weakens the claimed novelty and effectiveness. For a conference like ICLR, greater emphasis should also be placed on the learning algorithm’s performance and robustness, including how policy improvements are quantified beyond simulator fidelity. In its current state, the paper shows strong promise but requires more rigorous technical detail, especially on calibration and evaluation, to fully support its claims.

comments.title: Incorrect Discount Factor Description
comments.quote: A discount factor 1 reduces the value of future rewards amplifying the value of the near-term reward.
comments.explanation: The sentence claims that 'a discount factor 1' reduces the value of future rewards, which contradicts standard RL convention. In fact, a discount factor \(\gamma = 1\) assigns equal weight to all future rewards, preserving their full cumulative value. It is when \(\gamma < 1\) (e.g., 0.99) that future rewards are devalued. The intended message — that discounting emphasizes short-term rewards — is correct in spirit, but the specific statement misattributes the behavior to \(\gamma = 1\), which does the opposite. This conceptual error appears in Section 2 where the MDP framework is introduced, and no equation or correction resolves it. The related issue 'Ambiguous Discount Factor in Objective Function' was merged here because this version presents the core logical flaw more directly.

comments.title: Inconsistent Symbol in Reward Function Definition
comments.quote: _Rt_ ( _St, At, v_ )
comments.explanation: The reward function is defined as depending on state _St_, action _At_, and an additional parameter _v_, which is never defined in the text. While _v_ may represent exogenous variables like weather or occupancy, its absence from the formal definitions undermines reproducibility. The reliance on an unexplained input in a core equation constitutes underspecification. No other issue duplicates this exact problem.

comments.title: Incorrect Transition Probability Domain in MDP Definition
comments.explanation: The transition probability is defined as _p_: S × S × A → [0,1], which reverses the standard MDP convention of _p_: S × A × S → [0,1] or _p_(s' | s, a). This ordering implies a symmetric dependence on two states rather than a transition from state-action to next state. While the meaning is recoverable from later references to _S_{t+1}_, the formal definition is technically incorrect and risks misleading readers unfamiliar with MDPs. This is a notation inconsistency with foundational RL conventions.

comments.title: Contradiction in Simulator Fidelity Metric Definition
comments.quote: We define the spatial Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of _Z_ zones at timestep _t_ as: Where _Treal,t,z_ is the measured zone air temperature for zone _z_ at timestamp _t_ , and _Tsim,t,z_ = _C_ 1 _z_  _Cc_ =1 _z[T][t,c]_[ is the mean temperature of all control volumes] _[ C][z]_[in zone] _[ z]_[ at time] _[ t]_[.]
comments.explanation: The text claims to define the spatial MAE but omits the actual formula. While the simulated temperature _Tsim,t,z_ is partially described (and later clarified as an average over control volumes), the MAE equation itself is missing. This is not merely a formatting artifact — the lack of a formal expression for a central evaluation metric impedes reproducibility, especially given that results in Table 3 depend on it. The related issue 'Spatial MAE Definition Missing Formalization' was merged here because this entry includes both the missing formula and the critical context of evaluation fidelity.

comments.title: Underspecification of Air Mass Exchange Model via 'Randomized Shuffling'
comments.quote: We note that heat transfer using air circulation is effectively the exchange of air mass between control volumes, which we approximate by a randomized shuffling of air within thermal zones, parameterized by a shuffle probability.
comments.explanation: The 'randomized shuffling' model for air circulation is introduced as a key approximation but is severely underspecified. The mechanism of shuffling (e.g., neighbor swaps vs. global permutation), conservation of energy, spatial locality, and algorithmic implementation are not described. Terms like 'shuffle probability' and 'swap radius' (from Table 2) are used without definition, and the zero values for shuffle probability in tuning contradict claims that it was varied. Later issues ('Missing Definition of Shuffle Probability Model', 'Ambiguity in “randomized shuffling of air” as a convection model', 'Inconsistent notation for shuffle probability and radius') were merged here because this issue captures all aspects: vagueness, reproducibility risk, and parameter inconsistency.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Calibration' vs. 'Hyperparameter Calibration'
comments.quote: We ran hyperparameter tuning for 100 iterations on our simulator. Below are the parameters varied...
comments.explanation: The section titled 'Hyperparameter Calibration' describes the tuning of physical model parameters (e.g., thermal conductivity, convection coefficient), but these are not hyperparameters in the ML sense (like learning rate). This misuses standard terminology and risks confusing readers. While the broader use of 'calibration' for simulator fitting is acceptable, the label 'hyperparameter' is misleading. This reflects a conceptual misalignment in framing, though the method itself is sound.

comments.title: Claim of 'just over half a degree of drift' misrepresents evaluation results
comments.quote: On a two-story, 68,000 square foot building, with 127 devices, we were able to calibrate our simulator to have just over half a degree of drift from the real world over a 6 hour period.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'just over half a degree of drift', suggesting ~0.5 K, but Table 3 reports a test mean MAE of 0.83 K and individual errors up to 1.22 K. The term 'drift' is undefined and conflated with endpoint MAE, which is not cumulative drift. The claim may refer to a single training scenario (S1: 0.58 K), but this is not clarified. Related issues ('Claim of "just over half a degree" not clearly supported', 'Test Set MAE Appears Higher Than Claimed Drift', 'Undefined "drift" conflated with MAE') were merged here because this captures the full scope: misleading high-level claims, metric confusion, and lack of clarity about 'drift'.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated calibration metric and table results
comments.quote: The metric was calculated by obtaining the Mean Absolute Spatial Error of temperatures on each of the four train scenarios, and then averaging them.
comments.explanation: The text claims the calibration metric is the average MAE over four training scenarios (S1–S4), but quotes 0.58 K as the average — which matches only S1. The actual average is ~0.79 K, significantly higher. This misrepresents performance by citing the best-case training result as the average. While test error is correctly reported, this selective reporting constitutes a questionable claim about calibration fidelity.

comments.title: External convection coefficient compensates for radiative effects without acknowledgment
comments.quote: However, the external convection coefficient was higher than under the weather conditions, and likely is compensating for the radiative losses and gains, which were not directly simulated.
comments.explanation: The authors observe that the convection coefficient absorbs unmodeled radiative heat transfer but fail to present this as a key limitation earlier in the paper. Radiative effects are significant, and their omission — masked by parameter tuning — may compromise model robustness across weather conditions. While technically honest in diagnostics, the lack of upfront caveat weakens the claim of 'sufficient fidelity' for RL training, making this a questionable claim.

comments.title: Incorrect or ambiguous indexing in energy balance and dimensional description
comments.quote: thermal exchange across each face of the four participating faces control volume... This method divides each floor of the building into a grid of three-dimensional control volumes... simplified into a spatial two-dimensional heat transfer problem.
comments.explanation: This consolidated issue combines three closely related technical ambiguities: (1) the garbled phrase 'four participating faces control volume' likely means each control volume exchanges heat across four 2D faces; (2) the contradiction between 'three-dimensional control volumes' and 2D heat transfer is confusing, despite the adiabatic floor assumption; and (3) the energy balance equation lacks specification of boundary conditions (e.g., exterior walls, diffusers). Together, these create ambiguity about model dimensionality and boundary implementation, risking misinterpretation of spatial dynamics. The OCR hints do not excuse the lack of clarity in core physics modeling.

comments.title: Underspecification of Qext and HVAC-thermal coupling
comments.quote: an external energy source that enables applying local thermal energy from the HVAC model only for those control volumes that include an airflow diffuser, Qext
comments.explanation: The paper fails to specify how HVAC outputs (e.g., VAV supply temperature and airflow) are converted into localized heating/cooling (Qext) in control volumes. It claims thermal energy is 'equally distributed' to diffuser-equipped zones, which contradicts the proportionality to airflow stated earlier. Moreover, the link between shuffle probability and air mixing is unexplained. These omissions — coupled with lack of details on boundary conditions — render the coupling between HVAC and thermal model underspecified and physically implausible in parts.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of hat notation for action variables
comments.quote: We use hat notation to denote action variables.
comments.explanation: The paper states that hat (\hat{}) denotes action variables, but applies it to fixed system parameters like \hat{T}_{z,min} and \hat{T}_{z,max} that are not agent-controlled. Only \hat{T}_b and \hat{T}_s are actual actions. This contradicts the declared convention and misleads readers about which quantities are under agent control. The earlier 'Inconsistent use of hat notation for setpoints' was merged here because this version includes a more complete analysis of the logical inconsistency.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Q̇_s proportionality and VAV thermal distribution
comments.quote: Q̇_s ∝ ṁs × (Ts,out − Tz)
comments.explanation: The claim that thermal power is proportional to mass flow and temperature difference omits specific heat capacity (cp), though this is a minor issue. More importantly, the model assumes this energy is 'equally distributed' to all diffuser-equipped control volumes, which contradicts physical expectations — energy delivery should depend on airflow fraction. This simplification is not justified, and the inconsistency weakens the model's credibility. The related issues about Q̇_s and distribution were merged as they concern the same flawed HVAC-thermal coupling logic.

comments.title: Incorrect Unit Symbol for Temperature in MAE Metrics
comments.explanation: The paper reports MAE values using '_°K_', which is scientifically incorrect. The correct unit for temperature difference in SI is kelvin (K), without the degree symbol. Although the values likely represent °C-scale errors, the use of °K is deprecated and ambiguous. In building energy modeling, temperature differences are conventionally reported in °C. This misrepresentation — whether due to OCR or author error — deviates from field norms and risks confusion about scale and meaning.

comments.title: Missing or inconsistent reporting of median error in test results
comments.quote:
MAE
Median|...|NA
NA|
comments.explanation: The table reports median errors per test scenario (e.g., -0.29, 1.12) but labels 'Median' as 'NA' for training and test mean. This is inconsistent — if per-scenario medians are available, a summary (e.g., mean of medians or pooled median) should be computable. The omission creates ambiguity and suggests either a reporting error or incomplete analysis. While not a technical flaw, it reflects poor presentation that undermines metric transparency.

comments.title: Unjustified and underspecified future work claims
comments.quote: increasing the action space to include air handler and water system device on/off commands... Adding a Neural Network based predictor corrector to the tuning process...
comments.explanation: The proposed future extensions — hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces and neural predictor-corrector models — are introduced without addressing their complexity. The current RL framework uses SAC for continuous actions, but on/off commands require hybrid or hierarchical methods not discussed. Similarly, 'predictor corrector' is a vague, non-standard term in this context, with no details on integration, training, or architecture. These claims risk overselling future readiness and underspecify significant model changes, undermining the credibility of the research roadmap.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-structured and timely contribution to the application of reinforcement learning (RL) in real-world energy optimization problems, specifically focusing on HVAC control in commercial buildings. The motivation is strong, grounded in the significant carbon footprint of buildings and the limitations of current heuristic control policies. The authors propose a practical, scalable framework for offline RL training via a lightweight, calibrated building simulator—an important step toward deployable, data-efficient RL systems in safety- and cost-sensitive environments. The overall clarity is high, with a logical flow from problem formulation to methodology and preliminary results. Technical concepts are introduced with appropriate formalism, particularly in framing the control task as a Markov Decision Process and defining reward components relevant to energy, emissions, and comfort. However, the most significant issue lies in the incomplete development and justification of the calibration method: while the claim of "just over half a degree of drift over 6 hours" suggests high fidelity, the paper does not yet clarify how this calibration is achieved, what data it relies on, or how generalizable the approach is across buildings with heterogeneous systems or varying data quality. Additionally, the related work section cuts off mid-sentence, indicating an oversight in submission preparation. The absence of ablation studies or comparison with existing simulation approaches (e.g., EnergyPlus, data-driven emulators) weakens the claimed novelty and effectiveness. For a conference like ICLR, greater emphasis should also be placed on the learning algorithm’s performance and robustness, including how policy improvements are quantified beyond simulator fidelity. In its current state, the paper shows strong promise but requires more rigorous technical detail, especially on calibration and evaluation, to fully support its claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous Discount Factor in Objective Function
comments.explanation: At first glance, the summation symbol  appears corrupted by OCR — it likely should be \sum. Assuming that, the expression becomes \sum_t \gamma^t R(S_t, A_t), which aligns with the standard discounted cumulative reward in RL. However, the surrounding prose mentions 'a discount factor 1 reduces the value of future rewards', which is confusing: a discount factor of 1 does not reduce future rewards — it leaves them fully valued. The correct interpretation should be a discount factor \gamma \in (0,1), not 1. This appears to be a typographic error in the text, not the equation. The equation (once corrected for OCR) is standard: \theta^* = \arg\max_\theta \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_t \gamma^t R(S_t, A_t) \right]. But the prose misstatement — 'a discount factor 1' — could mislead a careful reader into thinking future rewards are not discounted, which contradicts the equation. This qualifies as a questionable claim because it misrepresents a key RL concept.

comments.title: Inconsistent Symbol in Reward Function Definition
comments.quote: _Rt_ ( _St, At, v_ )
comments.explanation: The reward function is defined as depending on state _St_, action _At_, and an additional parameter _v_. The text does not define _v_ anywhere in the provided context. Initially, I considered whether _v_ might be a typo from OCR — perhaps meant to be _t_ (time) or _s_ (for weather or sensor)? But in related works and simulator sections, no such variable is introduced. Later context in the full paper (not provided) might define _v_ as a vector of exogenous variables (e.g., weather, occupancy), which is common. However, in the absence of such clarification, the introduction of an unexplained variable in a core definition is ambiguous and could mislead: a reader might assume _v_ is a constant, or miss its significance in shaping the reward. Given the emphasis on energy and emissions, _v_ likely captures time-varying carbon intensity or weather, but omitting this in the definition weakens reproducibility. This counts as underspecified because the method relies on inputs that aren't defined.

comments.title: Incorrect Transition Probability Domain in MDP Definition
comments.explanation: The transition probability is defined as _p_ : S × S × A → [0,1], which is incorrect as written. The standard definition in MDPs is _p_: S × A × S → [0,1] or equivalently _p_(s' | s, a), modeling the probability of reaching state s' from state s under action a. The ordering S × S × A implies two states and an action — it would make more sense if it were S × A × S. This is not likely an OCR error, as the symbols appear consistent. Rather, it's a notation inconsistency with standard RL conventions (e.g., Sutton & Barto). Although the intended meaning is recoverable and the paper later refers correctly to 'next state S_{t+1}', the formal definition is technically wrong. This could mislead a reader unfamiliar with MDPs into thinking the transition depends symmetrically on two states, rather than being conditioned on a state-action pair to predict the next state.

comments.title: Contradiction in Simulator Fidelity Metric Definition
comments.quote: We define the spatial Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of _Z_ zones at timestep _t_ as: Where _Treal,t,z_ is the measured zone air temperature for zone _z_ at timestamp _t_ , and _Tsim,t,z_ = _C_ 1 _z_  _Cc_ =1 _z[T][t,c]_[ is the mean temperature of all control volumes] _[ C][z]_[in zone] _[ z]_[ at time] _[ t]_[.]
comments.explanation: The text introduces a formal definition of MAE but then fails to provide the actual formula — it says 'as:' and jumps straight to the explanation. This appears to be an OCR or formatting failure where the equation was dropped. The prose defines _Tsim,t,z_, the simulated mean temperature, but never states how MAE is computed. The logical conclusion is MAE = \frac{1}{Z}\sum_{z=1}^Z |T_{real,t,z} - T_{sim,t,z}|, which is standard, but without the actual formula, the definition is underspecified. Given the importance of evaluation metrics in simulation papers, omitting the explicit formula, even if conventional, impedes reproducibility. The rest of the section depends on this metric for calibration, so the missing expression is a critical omission.

comments.title: Mismatched Citation Count for RL Applications
comments.quote: (Yu et al., 2021; Mason & Grijalva, 2019; Yu et al., 2020; Gao & Wang, 2023; Wang et al., 2023; V´azquez-Canteli & Nagy, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b; Fang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019b)
comments.explanation: Zhang et al., 2019b is cited twice in the same list. This is likely a duplicate entry due to a citation error, possibly from merging multiple references with the same year and author but different titles. In standard academic practice, references should be unique. While not a technical flaw in modeling, this raises concerns about the rigor of the literature review. It could mislead readers into thinking there are nine distinct works when only eight are listed. However, this is a minor issue and falls just within the bounds of typographic error rather than logical or technical error — arguably, it does not rise to the level of a 'problem' if the intended works are still covered. Given that duplicate citations don't affect the technical argument, it is not flagged as a separate issue, but noted here for context. The paper otherwise surveys a broad and relevant set of works.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter symbol in temperature notation
comments.quote: _Treal,t,z_ is the measured zone air temperature for zone _z_ at timestamp _t_ , and _Tsim,t,z_ = _C_ 1 _z_  _Cc_ =1 _z[T][t,c]_[ is the mean temperature of all control volumes] _[ C][z]_[in zone] _[ z]_[ at time] _[ t]_[.]
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the notation _Tsim,t,z_ appears consistent with the earlier use of _T_b_ and _T_s_ as physical temperatures. However, the expression for _Tsim,t,z_ contains several OCR artifacts — notably '_C 1 _z_  _Cc_ =1 _z[T][t,c]_' — which clearly stems from a corrupted LaTeX expression. Given the context and standard convention, this was likely intended to be:

$$
